headline: Southeast Asia's Mangrove Forests Stage Remarkable Comeback
meta_description: After decades of devastating losses, Southeast Asia has reversed its mangrove decline, now accounting for 43% of global mangrove gains since 2010.
summary_short: Once the epicenter of mangrove destruction, Southeast Asia has transformed into a conservation success story in just over a decade.
body:
For nearly three decades, Southeast Asia hemorrhaged mangrove forests. Between 1980 and 2010, the region accounted for almost 60% of the world's mangrove losses, with the damage peaking between 1990 and 2005 as shrimp ponds and agricultural expansion consumed the coastlines.

But something shifted after 2010. A sweeping analysis of four decades of satellite data reveals that Southeast Asia has not only halted the decline—it has reversed it. Since 2010, the region has contributed roughly 43% of global mangrove gains, transforming from a cautionary tale into what researchers are calling a rare conservation success.

Indonesia, the world's most mangrove-rich nation, stopped experiencing steep forest losses after 2005. Myanmar, historically the most severely deforested among major mangrove countries, has seen a 10% increase in mangrove coverage since 2010. These shifts suggest that legal protections, public awareness boosted by the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami, and the trees' own resilience are working together.

The recovery happens naturally too: mangroves are colonizing abandoned shrimp ponds, like in Indonesia's Mahakam Delta, reclaiming land humans had written off. Yet researchers caution that these young forests cannot yet match the carbon storage or storm protection of ancient stands—healing, they remind us, takes time.
